title: The Sahel and Its Predecessors
bullets: The Sahel is a 600-to-1,000-mile-wide band of semi-arid grassland running 3,400 miles east-to-west between the Sahara and the savanna — the negotiation zone where every great West African empire from Ghana through Mali to Songhai grew up.

The Niger River bends north into the Sahel and then comes back south, creating the Inner Niger Delta — 15,000 square miles of seasonally flooded grassland that became the agricultural breadbasket of the Mali Empire.

The Ghana Empire — called Wagadu by its own Soninke-speaking people — controlled the western Sahel gold-and-salt trade for roughly five centuries from a capital at Koumbi Saleh, with eleventh-century Arab geographer al-Bakri describing kings adorned with gold necklaces and gold-harnessed horses.

The traditional account of an Almoravid sack of Ghana in 1076 is now disputed by modern historians, who see Ghana's collapse as a slower process driven by ecological change, internal fragmentation, and accumulated Almoravid pressure rather than a single conquest.

Into the post-Ghana vacuum stepped the Sosso kingdom under King Sumanguru Kante, the sorcerer-king of the Sundiata epic whose seven-storied palace was said to have walls of human skin and who derived his power from blood sacrifice and bound spirits.

Mandinka social organization was built around named patrilineal clans — Keita (royal), Konde, Traore, Camara, Kone — each with inherited occupations, plus the joking-cousin sanankuya relationships allowing structured insults across clan lines as conflict resolution.
Camel caravans linked Bambuk and Bure gold from south of the Sahel to Taghaza salt from the central Sahara, and at certain stages of the trade salt and gold reportedly traded weight-for-weight.

When Sundiata was a child, Genghis Khan had just died, the Mongol invasions were beginning, the Almohad Caliphate of Morocco was at its peak, and the Abbasid caliphate at Baghdad was twenty-three years from being destroyed by the Mongols.
subtitle: geography, the Niger River, the Ghana Empire, and the world Sundiata inherited

title: Sundiata and the Founding
bullets: In or around 1235 CE, Sundiata Keita defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru at the Battle of Kirina, ending Sosso domination of the upper Niger and establishing the Mali Empire that would dominate West Africa for two centuries.

The legendary Sundiata is a sickly child who could not walk until age seven, an exile who learned hunting in Mema, a chosen king prophesied by a hunchback hunter-diviner, and the warrior who killed Sumanguru with an arrow tipped with a white rooster's spur.

The Kouyate clan are still the official jeli to the Keita lineage today, eight centuries after their ancestor Bala Faseke chanted the first Sundiata epic at the founding court — the continuity runs across nearly thirty generations.

The Kurukan Fuga, also called the Manden Charter, was promulgated in 1236 as an oral constitution governing the empire's clans, establishing rights, prohibiting certain forms of slavery, and protecting refuge for displaced people.

Magna Carta was forced on King John at Runnymede about twenty years before the Kurukan Fuga; the two documents were produced by completely independent political traditions with no contact between their authors.

Sundiata established Niani as capital — a city Ibn Battuta would describe a century later, with a royal compound surrounded by wooden palisade and an ebony throne with elephant tusks at the corners and golden vessels arranged before it.

Mali's three-ring administrative structure kept subject peoples autonomous and culturally distinct rather than assimilating them, allowing the empire to absorb Wolof, Tukulor, Soninke, Songhai, Dogon, and Bozo populations while extracting tribute and military service.

The same year Sundiata convened the Kurukan Fuga, Ögedei Khan launched the western Mongol invasion of Russia and Eastern Europe, Razia Sultana ruled the Sultanate of Delhi, and the Hojo regents controlled Japan from Kamakura.
subtitle: the Lion King, the Battle of Kirina, and an oral constitution older than Magna Carta is written

title: The Mansas Consolidate
bullets: The Inner Niger Delta supports African rice — Oryza glaberrima, independently domesticated in West Africa around 1500 BCE, more than three thousand years before Mali emerged — along the receding annual flood, plus pearl millet, sorghum, fonio, cowpeas, and groundnuts.

Mali cavalry could exist only because the empire sat north of the tsetse fly belt, where horses can survive — a trained warhorse imported from North Africa cost the equivalent of several enslaved people, making cavalry an expensive aristocratic monopoly.

The jeli were a hereditary caste of musicians, oral historians, genealogists, and political advisors whose institutional protection across regime changes preserved knowledge that would otherwise have been lost — the Sundiata epic survived eight centuries because the institution survived.

Mansa Uli, Sundiata's son, made the first recorded Malian pilgrimage to Mecca in the 1260s, establishing the precedent that Mali's mansas would routinely make the hajj as a diplomatic act as well as a religious one.

Mansa Sakura was a freed slave-soldier, not a member of the Keita royal lineage, who seized the throne around 1285 — a non-royal commander could become mansa under the right circumstances and his reign would still count as legitimate.

According to al-Umari, Mansa Musa told his Cairo hosts that his predecessor Abu Bakr II had abdicated to lead 2,000 ships into the Atlantic — modern historians treat the voyage as essentially legendary rather than documented.

Estimates of Mali Empire population are speculative but archaeological evidence points to several hundred populated settlements in the Inner Delta alone, with empire-wide population running into the millions by the early fourteenth century — comparable to contemporary England or France.

The Bozo and Somono fishermen lived in stilt villages along the Niger and traded smoke-dried catfish, perch, and tilapia inland to gold-mining regions hundreds of miles away that had no year-round access to fresh fish.
subtitle: Niger flood agriculture, the cavalry-horse system, the jeli institution, and the early successors

title: Mansa Musa
bullets: In spring 1324, a caravan of around 60,000 people including twelve thousand slaves carrying gold ingots and a hundred camels each loaded with several hundred pounds of gold dust left Mali heading for Cairo and Mecca — the most spectacular hajj in the history of Islam.

Mansa Musa was the first Mali ruler who appears to have been a deeply observant practitioner of Islam — he prayed five times a day, observed Ramadan, and undertook the hajj as a personal religious obligation rather than primarily as a diplomatic act.

Cairo's bimetallic currency system collapsed during Musa's three-month stay in 1324: the gold-to-silver exchange rate fell sharply and stayed depressed for ten to twelve years before recovering, the best-documented example of a pre-modern monetary shock.

The Catalan Atlas of 1375, drawn by the Mallorcan Jewish cartographer Abraham Cresques for King Charles V of France, shows Mansa Musa enthroned holding a gold nugget — the first known appearance of a sub-Saharan African ruler on a European map.

The popular ranking of Mansa Musa as the wealthiest person in human history is essentially unverifiable, since no medieval economy produced records that would let you compare wealth across centuries; what is true is that he had access to more gold than any other ruler of his era.

Musa returned with the Andalusian poet-architect al-Sahili, traditionally credited with the Djinguereber Mosque in Timbuktu — though the indigenous Sahelian mud-brick tradition was actually centuries older than al-Sahili's arrival.

Musa borrowed money at high interest from Cairo merchants to fund the return journey after his attendants were systematically overcharged by Cairo merchants who realized they could exploit the gold-gifting caravan — some creditors followed him to Mali to collect.

The same decade Musa was completing the Djinguereber Mosque in Timbuktu, Edward II of England was deposed and probably murdered, the Hundred Years' War began, the Black Death was about to arrive in Europe, and the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was just being founded.
subtitle: the most famous African ruler in pre-modern history and the gold-crash that put Mali on European maps

title: Timbuktu and Sankoré
bullets: Timbuktu sits about 8 miles north of the Niger River on the southern edge of the Sahara, founded sometime in the eleventh or twelfth century probably as a seasonal Tuareg encampment — the name is generally believed to derive from a Tuareg word meaning place of Tin Buktu.

The three great mosques of Timbuktu are the Djinguereber, the Sankoré, and the Sidi Yahya — all mud-brick buildings with characteristic projecting palm-wood toron beams that double as built-in scaffolding for the regular re-plastering each rainy season requires.

The Sankoré complex is sometimes called Sankoré University, but the structure was less institutionalized than the modern term implies — there was no central administration, no fixed faculty, no degrees, only a community of scholars working in proximity.

Modern estimates place the Timbuktu manuscript corpus at somewhere between several hundred thousand and a million individual surviving works — by far the largest body of pre-colonial sub-Saharan African writing in existence.

Ibn Battuta — the most widely traveled person of the pre-modern period, with 75,000 miles by his own account — spent eight months in Mali in 1352–53 and produced the most detailed eyewitness description of the empire at its functional peak.

Ibn Battuta thought Mali's women had insufficient regard for proper Islamic modesty — many appeared in public unveiled, mixed freely with unrelated men, and held positions of influence in ways his Maghrebi sensibility found shocking.
Sonni Ali of Songhai captured Timbuktu in 1468; he was famously violent toward the city's scholarly class, and his name remained a byword for impiety in Timbuktu's later chronicles.

Ahmed Baba al-Timbukti wrote more than forty books on Islamic law and biography, was deported to Marrakesh after the 1591 Moroccan invasion, and argued in detail that the enslavement of West African Muslims was prohibited by Islamic law — a sophisticated legal critique of the very trade Mali had facilitated.
subtitle: three mosques, hundreds of thousands of manuscripts, and what Timbuktu actually was

title: Songhai Begins to Eat Mali
bullets: Songhai is a linguistic isolate or possible Nilo-Saharan family member, distinct from the Niger-Congo family that includes Mandinka — the Songhai-speakers had been on the Niger Bend around Gao for centuries before Mali existed.

Sonni Ali ruled Songhai from 1464 to 1492 — almost exactly the years of the early European voyages of discovery — and is one of the great empire-builders in Sahelian memory and a violent tyrant in the Timbuktu chronicles, both views partly correct.

The capture of Timbuktu in 1468 was the structural turning point of fifteenth-century West African history; the loss of the Niger Bend cut Mali off from the Saharan trade routes that had been its economic foundation.

The siege of Djenné in 1473 lasted either seven years and seven months in the chronicler-friendly version or some shorter period in the modern-historian version, eventually starving the city into surrender.
Sonni Ali died in 1492 — the year Columbus reached the Caribbean and the Catholic Monarchs completed the Reconquista — under unclear circumstances, possibly drowning while crossing a river.

Askia Muhammad seized the Songhai throne in 1493 and made his own deliberate-echo hajj in 1497–98, receiving the honorific title of Caliph of Sudan from the Abbasid puppet caliph in Cairo — formal Islamic credentials no Mali mansa had bothered to obtain.

The Maghrebi scholar al-Maghili wrote Replies to Askia in response to Askia Muhammad's request for guidance on how a Muslim ruler should conduct his government — one of the most sophisticated political documents to come out of medieval West Africa.

Mali in 1530 was no longer an empire in the territorial sense, but the Keita lineage continued, the Kurukan Fuga continued, the jeli continued, and the Mande language network still extended across an enormous swath of West Africa from the Atlantic to the upper Niger.
subtitle: Sonni Ali, Askia Muhammad, and the Mande core that survived the eclipse

title: The Saadian Strike
bullets: At Tondibi on March 13, 1591, an army of approximately 4,000 Moroccan soldiers — most carrying matchlock muskets, supported by medium cannons — defeated a Songhai army of perhaps 40,000 to 50,000 in a single afternoon.

Ahmad al-Mansur of Morocco used the Portuguese ransom money from the 1578 Battle of the Three Kings to build a substantial army equipped with matchlock muskets bought from Ottoman trade and European arms dealers.

The Saadian expedition was led by Judar Pasha, a Spanish-born eunuch who had been captured as a child by Saadian raiders, converted to Islam, raised in al-Mansur's household, and risen to senior military command.

The five-month Saharan crossing from October 1590 to March 1591 killed roughly half of the original 4,000-man Saadian force, with almost all the cavalry mounts dying en route — what arrived at the Niger was a small, hardened, exhausted force with several hundred functional firearms.

Tondibi was the first time gunpowder weapons decisively determined a major engagement in sub-Saharan Africa, opening the technological gap that would underwrite European colonial conquest of the Sahel three centuries later.

The Arma — descendants of the original Saadian musketeers — settled in Timbuktu and Gao, married locally, and within a generation were ruling the Niger Bend cities as an autonomous hereditary military caste, with formal Marrakesh authority attenuated to a periodic exchange of ceremonial gifts.

Al-Mansur died of plague in 1603; the Saadian dynasty fragmented within a decade; the gold flow that 1591 was supposed to deliver to Marrakesh collapsed back to pre-invasion levels — a classic tactical victory that produced no useful strategic result.

Mali was not directly attacked but the structural shock of Songhai's collapse disrupted the trans-Saharan trade Mali depended on, and by the 1620s Mali was a small Mande kingdom of regional importance rather than an imperial state.
subtitle: Morocco's matchlock musketeers cross the Sahara and finish the great Sahelian empires

title: The Long Mali Tail
bullets: After the imperial center disintegrated, the Keita royal family relocated to Kangaba on the upper Niger — the original Keita homeland Sundiata's family had come from before the Sosso conflict drove them into exile.

Every seven years the Kamabolon ceremony at Kangaba reroofs a small mud-and-thatch shrine while selected jeli of the Diabate, Kouyate, and other senior griot lineages recite the full Sundiata epic — the canonical text performed by institutionally recognized custodians.

The Kaabu Federation in modern Guinea-Bissau, Senegal, and Guinea outlasted the Mali imperial center by nearly two and a half centuries, ending only at the Battle of Kansala in 1867 when the Fula imamate of Futa Jallon defeated the last Kaabu Mansaba.

Ibn Battuta described imperial Niani in 1352 in detail, but the city has not been securely identified by modern archaeologists — Sahelian mud-brick cities dissolve back into the landscape within decades when maintenance stops.

In 2012, an alliance of Tuareg separatist forces and al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb seized Timbuktu and began burning manuscripts; librarian Abdel Kader Haidara organized a clandestine network that smuggled an estimated 350,000 manuscripts to Bamako in metal trunks, donkey carts, and rice sacks.

UNESCO inscribed the Manden Charter on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2009, formal acknowledgment of what the jeli had been preserving for nearly eight centuries.

The first European to reach Timbuktu and return alive was the Frenchman René Caillié in 1828, who found a sun-baked clay town that fell well short of the eighteenth-century European legend — the myth had outlived the imperial city by three centuries.

The modern Republic of Mali, which took the imperial name on independence in 1960, looks back to the Mali Empire as its symbolic ancestor without claiming any direct institutional descent — the cultural project has lasted nearly twice as long as the political empire did.
subtitle: Kangaba, the Kamabolon ceremony, the manuscript rescue, and what the empire became
